findings.rule: check_writing.md §7 (always you, never the student) + check_student_facing.md §26 (no teacher framing in body)
findings.title: Third-person pattern naming in cautionary context
findings.description: Line 29 uses third-person framing: 'Business people who have just seen multi-agent work want to split everything.' Reads as naming a common industry temptation rather than narrating the student's state. Immediately followed by direct address ('Resist it'). Acceptable under equals-not-pupils principle when used for pattern-naming, not student-behavior narration.
findings.rationale: The sentence names a common pattern the student might encounter (business stakeholders pushing for multi-agent solutions), not describing what the student will do or has done. Paired with 'Resist it,' it lands as peer caution. Does not violate the equals-not-pupils principle because it names an external temptation, not student inadequacy. No edit required.
